news_summary: Today's positive news highlights advancements in digital rights across Africa, the cultural significance of Galician identity and literature, and China's rising contributions to global research through increased author participation.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière les progrès réalisés en matière de droits numériques à travers l'Afrique, l'importance culturelle de l'identité et de la littérature galiciennes, ainsi que la contribution croissante de la Chine à la recherche mondiale grâce à une participation accrue des auteurs.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy ponen de relieve los avances en materia de derechos digitales en toda África, la importancia cultural de la identidad y la literatura gallegas, y la creciente contribución de China a la investigación mundial gracias a una mayor participación de los autores.

articles.title: The signal we cannot ignore: What the LONDA 2025 report reveals about Africa’s digital rights
articles.summary: The digital landscape is becoming increasingly dangerous for vulnerable groups. Technology-facilitated gender-based violence is a growing epidemic that most legal frameworks fail to address adequately.
articles.content:
Paradigm Initiative recently published the LONDA 2025 report, exposing the fragile state of digital rights across 29 African countries. While a few nations enacted progressive laws to protect user data, many governments intensified internet shutdowns, surveillance, and arrests to control online spaces. The comprehensive document highlights how digital democracy is expanding in some regions while facing severe authoritarian pushback in others.

The annual report evaluates country compliance with international human rights standards using a score index. Researchers assess 12 key indicators, including internet shutdowns, digital inclusion, false news criminalization, data protection, and child online safety. They assign scores out of 60 to gauge how well governments protect the freedoms outlined in the African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights (ACHPR) Declaration.

A continent divided: Regional score shifts

The LONDA 2025 report reveals stark contrasts in how different regions handle digital freedoms. By comparing 2024 and 2025 scores, researchers noted both significant legislative leaps and concerning democratic regressions across the continent.

In Southern Africa, Botswana emerged as a notable success story. The country jumped from a score of 28 in 2024 to 35 in 2025. Lawmakers implemented the Data Protection Act and the Access to Information Act, strengthening citizens’ privacy rights. Furthermore, Botswana maintained a perfect record of zero internet shutdowns. Namibia also made strides by rolling out its Universal Service Fund (USF), which provided 4G access to more than 9,000 citizens for the first time. However, neighboring Mozambique barely improved, rising from 23 to 24, as authorities approved regulations allowing telecommunications suspensions during perceived threats to public security.

However, progress in one nation does not guarantee regional safety, as seen in Zambia. The country maintained a relatively stable digital landscape throughout 2025. Yet, early in 2026, the Zambian government suddenly forced a last-minute postponement of RightsCon, the world’s largest gathering of human rights defenders in the digital age. In reality, this unexpected decision amounted to a complete cancellation of the massive event, catching organizers and attendees off guard less than a week before it was scheduled to begin. Shutting the door on a monumental global event is a sudden and severe restriction of civic space. It directly contradicts any prior progress Zambia claimed to have made. Digital rights advocates are already questioning how far Zambia’s score will plummet in the upcoming 2026 review. This incident proves that a government can undo years of digital rights progress with a single political decision.

West Africa presented a mixed bag of progress and backsliding. Senegal saw its score rise from 34 to 38. The ECOWAS Court of Justice issued a landmark ruling condemning the Senegalese state for its 2023 internet blackouts, establishing a vital legal precedent for digital rights. Consequently, the Senegalese government kept the internet on throughout 2025. Conversely, Togo’s score dropped from 29 to 28. During civic protests in June 2025, Togolese authorities severely disrupted access to platforms such as WhatsApp, Facebook, and Telegram.

Nigeria experienced a slight dip, moving from 36 to 35. While the Nigerian Data Protection Commission actively enforced privacy laws, the cost of telecommunications services increased for the first time since 2013, and opaque content takedowns remained a persistent issue. The Gambia achieved moderate compliance, notably enacting a data protection law in November 2025, but it still lacks a functional USF after 15 years of promises.

In North Africa, Egypt climbed from a score of 32 to 38. The government launched the second edition of its National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Strategy and adopted an open data policy. Despite this progress, existing laws still permit surveillance for indefinite periods without clear safeguards.

Further south, Sudan dropped to an abysmal score of 20 from 21 in 2024. The ongoing conflict decimated connectivity, with authorities shutting down the internet during exams and armed groups seizing citizens’ electronic devices to inspect private communications. In East Africa, Kenya recorded internet throttling during a period of unrest in June and restricted Telegram during national exams. Tanzania also plunged its citizens into a total digital blackout during its October elections, accompanied by over 1,700 arrests and restricting access to the social media platform X.

The ongoing struggle against shutdowns and censorship

At the continental level, the African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights (ACHPR) is actively pushing back against digital repression.

In 2024, the commission passed Resolution 580, which explicitly urges states to keep the internet open and secure before, during, and after elections. Building on this, they passed Resolution 630 in 2025, which guides tech companies on maintaining information integrity and conducting transparent human rights impact assessments. Despite these clear directives, internet shutdowns remain a favored tool for several African governments facing political tension.

The LONDA report documented deliberate network disruptions in multiple nations. In Cameroon, the government cut off electronic communication systems between October 23 and November 7. This disruption coincided directly with the delayed announcement of the 2025 presidential election results and severe political tension.

When authorities do not shut down the internet entirely, they often use restrictive legislation to target dissenting voices. Across the continent, governments weaponize penal codes and cybercrime laws to silence journalists and activists under the guise of combating false news. In Benin, the application of the Digital Code resulted in the arbitrary arrest of several newspaper editors and digital activists who criticized the administration.

Similarly, Somalia security forces frequently targeted journalists, confiscating their electronic devices without formal charges. In Ghana, authorities continue to misapply the Electronic Communications Act to arrest and harass media professionals, even though the country repealed its criminal libel laws years ago. These actions directly violate international human rights law and create a chilling effect that forces citizens to self-censor.

Surveillance, privacy, and the gender divide

The digital landscape is becoming increasingly dangerous for vulnerable groups. Technology-facilitated gender-based violence (TFGBV) is a growing epidemic that most legal frameworks fail to address adequately. The report highlighted severe gendered disinformation campaigns in Benin, specifically aimed at keeping women out of politics. Attackers spread false narratives relying on misogyny to demean women leaders and discourage female political participation.

Surveillance technologies also pose a massive threat to privacy across Africa. In Zimbabwe, Lesotho, and Togo, citizens face targeted communications surveillance. In Togo, the government deployed a new cybersecurity center, creating a climate of fear and pushing journalists and youth to self-censor their online communications. Most nations lack the necessary judicial oversight to prevent intelligence agencies from abusing these monitoring tools.

To bridge the digital divide, many countries established Universal Service Funds (USF) to finance connectivity in rural and low-income areas. While Botswana and Malawi actively used these funds to connect rural schools, many other nations manage their funds with zero transparency. The lack of robust USF implementation leaves marginalized communities, including people with disabilities, locked out of the digital economy.

There is a glimmer of hope regarding child online safety. In 2025, Kenya issued industry guidelines for child online protection, Lesotho launched similar guidelines developed in 2024, and Tunisia adopted the National Charter for the Protection of Children Online. However, most countries still lack specific laws protecting children from cyberbullying, online grooming, and data profiling. Artificial intelligence governance is also slowly taking root, with Kenya, Zimbabwe, and Zambia developing National AI strategies in 2025 to guide future technological integration.

Amplifying the message through film

Reports and data are vital for documentation, but changing hearts and minds requires compelling storytelling. To bring the struggles documented in these pages to life, Paradigm Initiative produced the short film “Signal.” Extracted directly from the findings of the LONDA 2024 report, the movie sheds light on the hidden costs of exclusion for vulnerable and rural communities left on the margins of the digital world. It demonstrates that for these people, the most important messages are often the ones that struggle to get through. Every plot point in the film mirrors the exact realities citizens faced across the continent over the past two years.

When digital democracy disappears, so does the power of the people. The LONDA 2025 report serves as a critical warning and a roadmap. While a few African nations are building inclusive, rights-respecting digital economies, too many others are constructing digital architectures of control.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the LONDA 2025 report which provides a comprehensive, data-driven evaluation of digital rights across 29 African countries, highlighting both progress and setbacks. It details significant legislative improvements in countries like Botswana and Senegal that enhance digital privacy and inclusion, as well as efforts by the African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights to combat digital repression. The report also addresses critical issues such as technology-facilitated gender-based violence, internet shutdowns, surveillance, and digital exclusion, emphasizing the broad societal impact on millions of citizens. This represents a meaningful, substantive advancement in protecting and promoting digital rights and freedoms across a large and diverse population, making it genuinely inspiring and hopeful for digital democracy and human rights in Africa.
articles.personality_title: LONDA 2025 report shows progress and challenges for digital rights in Africa
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The LONDA 2025 report by Paradigm Initiative reviews how 29 African countries protect digital rights like privacy, internet access, and freedom of expression. It uses a detailed scoring system to measure government actions against international human rights standards.

**What happened** – The report found that some countries, like Botswana and Senegal, made important improvements by passing new laws and keeping the internet open. Botswana strengthened data protection and information access laws, while Senegal avoided internet blackouts after a court ruling. However, other countries still face problems like internet shutdowns, censorship, and surveillance. For example, Zambia unexpectedly canceled a major human rights event, and Tanzania blocked internet access during elections.

**Impact** – This report is important because it gives clear evidence of where digital rights are improving and where they are at risk. It shows that progress in one country can be undone quickly by political decisions, and highlights ongoing threats like gender-based online violence and government spying. The report also points to efforts by the African Commission on Human and Peoples’ Rights to protect digital freedoms, showing a growing movement to fight back against repression.

**What's next step** – Countries will be watched closely in the 2026 report to see if they continue improving or fall behind. The report encourages governments to stop internet shutdowns, improve laws protecting vulnerable groups, and make internet access fairer. The use of Universal Service Funds to connect rural areas could grow, helping more people join the digital world safely.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The LONDA 2025 report highlights both hopeful progress and serious challenges in protecting digital rights across Africa, urging stronger laws and open internet for all.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rapport LONDA 2025 montre des progrès et des défis pour les droits numériques en Afrique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rapport LONDA 2025, publié par Paradigm Initiative, évalue la protection des droits numériques dans 29 pays africains, comme la vie privée, l’accès à Internet et la liberté d’expression. Il utilise un système de notation détaillé pour mesurer les actions des gouvernements selon les normes internationales des droits humains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le rapport révèle que certains pays, comme le Botswana et le Sénégal, ont réalisé des avancées importantes en adoptant de nouvelles lois et en maintenant l’accès à Internet. Le Botswana a renforcé ses lois sur la protection des données et l’accès à l’information, tandis que le Sénégal a évité les coupures d’Internet après une décision de justice. Cependant, d’autres pays rencontrent encore des problèmes, notamment des coupures d’Internet, la censure et la surveillance. Par exemple, la Zambie a annulé de façon inattendue un grand événement sur les droits humains, et la Tanzanie a bloqué l’accès à Internet lors des élections.

**Impact** – Ce rapport est important car il montre clairement où les droits numériques progressent et où ils sont menacés. Il démontre qu’un progrès peut être rapidement annulé par des décisions politiques et met en lumière des menaces comme la violence en ligne basée sur le genre et la surveillance gouvernementale. Le rapport souligne aussi les efforts de la Commission africaine des droits de l’homme pour protéger ces libertés numériques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays seront de nouveau évalués dans le rapport de 2026 pour suivre les progrès ou les reculs. Le rapport encourage les gouvernements à cesser les coupures d’Internet, à améliorer les lois pour les groupes vulnérables et à rendre l’accès à Internet plus équitable. L’utilisation des Fonds de service universel pour connecter les zones rurales pourrait se développer, aidant plus de personnes à accéder au monde numérique en toute sécurité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le rapport LONDA 2025 montre à la fois des progrès encourageants et des défis majeurs pour la protection des droits numériques en Afrique, appelant à des lois plus fortes et à un Internet ouvert pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: El informe LONDA 2025 muestra avances y desafíos en los derechos digitales en África
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El informe LONDA 2025, publicado por Paradigm Initiative, evalúa cómo 29 países africanos protegen los derechos digitales como la privacidad, el acceso a Internet y la libertad de expresión. Utiliza un sistema de puntuación detallado para medir las acciones de los gobiernos según los estándares internacionales de derechos humanos.

**Qué pasó** – El informe encontró que algunos países, como Botsuana y Senegal, lograron avances importantes al aprobar nuevas leyes y mantener el acceso a Internet. Botsuana fortaleció las leyes de protección de datos y acceso a la información, mientras que Senegal evitó cortes de Internet tras una decisión judicial. Sin embargo, otros países enfrentan problemas como apagones de Internet, censura y vigilancia. Por ejemplo, Zambia canceló inesperadamente un gran evento de derechos humanos, y Tanzania bloqueó el acceso a Internet durante las elecciones.

**Impacto** – Este informe es importante porque muestra claramente dónde los derechos digitales están mejorando y dónde están en riesgo. Demuestra que un progreso puede revertirse rápidamente por decisiones políticas y destaca amenazas como la violencia de género en línea y la vigilancia gubernamental. El informe también resalta los esfuerzos de la Comisión Africana de Derechos Humanos para proteger estas libertades digitales.

**Próximo paso** – Los países serán observados de cerca en el informe de 2026 para ver si continúan mejorando o retroceden. El informe anima a los gobiernos a detener los apagones de Internet, mejorar las leyes para grupos vulnerables y hacer que el acceso a Internet sea más justo. El uso de Fondos de Servicio Universal para conectar áreas rurales podría crecer, ayudando a más personas a unirse al mundo digital de forma segura.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El informe LONDA 2025 destaca tanto el progreso alentador como los desafíos serios en la protección de los derechos digitales en África, instando a leyes más fuertes y a un Internet abierto para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an African digital landscape shaped like a glowing network map connecting diverse rural villages and urban centers, with vibrant, illuminated pathways symbolizing open internet access and data flow, contrasted by shadowy silhouettes of locked gates and dimmed communication towers representing censorship and shutdowns, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: ‘Galician identity and literature are deeply rooted in land,’ award-winning Galician writer explains
articles.summary: Galician literature not only narrates, but constructs identity. It is a form of belonging, projecting oneself and inhabiting the world.
articles.content:
Every May 17, the autonomous region of Galicia, Spain, interrupts its routine to pay tribute to its precious language and literature. Galician Literature Day, celebrated since 1963, is not just a commemorative date, but a declaration of principles. In a community where culture has always been an act of resistance, remembering and creation, celebrating those who write – and sing – in Galician reaffirms a rich, complex and constantly changing collective identity.

With approximately 3,000,000 speakers mainly concentrated in the northeast of the Iberian peninsula, modern-day Galicia serves as an anchor of identity in a world that views multilingualism as an obstacle to progress rather than a means of empowerment.

In 2025, the day was dedicated to historic cantareiras (folk singers) from different regions of Galicia: the sisters Adolfina and Rosa Casás Rama (Cerceda), Eva Castiñeira (Muxía) and the group Pandereiteiras de Mens (Malpica de Bergantiños): Prudencia Garrido Ameijende, Asunción Garrido Ameijende and Manuela Lema Villar. Cantareiras are a genre of folk music sung by women and accompanied only by tambourines.

Galicia is, in many ways, an exemplary exception within the European geo-cultural map. In the theoretical framework that I developed with Sebastián Moreno Barreneche, geo-cultural identities signify a belonging to a cultural entity anchored by territory, and can take different forms: national, subnational, supranational, or transnational.

The case of Galicia demonstrates how an identity anchored in land can be built with a multifaceted outlook: a living language of its own, a literature that functions as a symbolic and emotional repository, and a diaspora that has managed to take Galician culture beyond the Atlantic without diluting its essence. Far from being encapsulated in a static concept of nation or folklore, Galician culture reinvents itself from its peripheral position while continuing to engage in dialogue with Celtic stories, migrant history, and the margins of the Spanish state, with the right to narrate itself from within.

To delve deeper into these topics and celebrate the occasion, we are publishing an interview conducted in July 2023 with award-winning writer and essayist María López Sández. The conversation took place at the Café Derby in Santiago de Compostela, a legendary venue for Galician gatherings located at the entrance to the historic centre. López Sández’s perspective helps us understand why literature is, for Galicia, much more than an artistic expression: it is a way of belonging, projecting oneself, and inhabiting the world.

Global Voices (GV): What role does literature play in the construction of Galician identity?

María López Sández (MLS): Es una herramienta clave, porque la producción cultural en general resulta decisiva para crear imaginarios que condicionan cómo las personas se perciben a sí mismas. Esto sucede en todos los contextos, pero en las literaturas minorizadas es aún más evidente, ya que suelen ocupar posiciones periféricas. Por eso, los procesos de desmitificación y relectura identitaria [NdR: entender la cultura propia como forma de hablar de ‘un quiénes somos’ colectivo] son más frágiles y complejos.

María López Sández (MLS): It is a key tool because the production of culture in general is vital in creating the collective imagination, which affects how people perceive themselves. This happens in all contexts, but in minority literatures it is even more evident, since they tend to occupy peripheral spaces. For this reason, the processes of demystification and identity re-reading [understanding one’s own culture as a way of speaking about a collective ‘us’] are more fragile and complex.

GV: In what ways can institutions protect a language like Galician?

MLS: Existen diversas vías. Es fundamental garantizar su presencia en los ámbitos académico, político, administrativo y educativo. Si las instituciones fuesen coherentes con lo que establece la Carta Europea de las Lenguas Regionales o Minoritarias y con el enfoque ecolingüístico que subyace en proyectos como el Archivo Digital de Lenguas en Peligro de Extinción, sin duda se producirían avances significativos. Es preciso asumir la defensa de la lengua propia como un proyecto colectivo de máxima trascendencia. En Timor Oriental, por ejemplo, con una complejidad lingüística enorme, se apostó por la enseñanza inicial en cada una de las lenguas del territorio. Y eso en un Estado muy reciente —fundado en 2002— y con circunstancias económicas mucho más adversas que las de Galicia. La situación del gallego no es de las más extremas, pero cada lengua representa un compromiso con la diversidad cultural del planeta. Comprometerse con la diversidad lingüística del mundo pasa por hablar y defender, en cada lugar, la lengua propia.

MLS: There are many ways. It is fundamental to guarantee their presence in academic, political, administrative and educational spheres. If institutions were aligned with what was established in the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages and with the ecolinguistic focus that is highlighted in projects such as the Digital Archive of Languages in Danger of Extinction, undoubtedly this would lead to significant advances. It is necessary to accept the defence of one’s own language as a collective project of maximum importance. In East Timor, for example, with its enormous linguistic complexity, the focus was on early childhood education in each of the territory’s languages. And this is in a very new country — founded in 2002 — and in much more adverse economic circumstances than Galicia. The situation of Galician is not one of the more extreme, but each language represents a commitment to the cultural diversity of the planet. Committing to global linguistic diversity happens by speaking and defending one’s own language everywhere.

GV: In linguistics, they talk about the importance of normalisation and standardisation of a language. What is the difference between these two concepts?

MLS: La normalización busca garantizar la presencia y el prestigio de la lengua en todos los ámbitos de la vida social. La normativización, por su parte, consiste en definir una variedad estándar que permita su uso en contextos formales y funcionales diversos. Son procesos interconectados e interdependientes que se desarrollan en paralelo en todas aquellas lenguas que aspiran a mantenerse vivas y operativas.

MLS: Normalisation seeks to guarantee the presence and prestige of a language in all areas of social life. Standardisation, on the other hand, consists of defining a standard variety which allows it to be used in diverse formal and functional contexts. They are interconnected and interdependent processes that develop in parallel in all languages that aspire to remain alive and functioning.

GV: In your book Paisaxe e nación (Landscape and nation) you propose that territory and personality are connected in Galician literature. How does this connection arise?

MLS: Es un fenómeno que también se da en otras culturas. En el siglo XIX, con el [movimiento cultural y artístico del] Romanticismo, se produjo una profunda revalorización de los paisajes, especialmente de aquellos en los que la presencia humana era menor: los más salvajes, alejados de lo social. Por eso se pasó del parque francés al jardín inglés, y espacios hasta entonces considerados hostiles —el mar, el desierto, la alta montaña— empezaron a valorarse desde la categoría de lo sublime. En Galicia, quien lleva a cabo ese proceso [en el siglo XIX] es Rosalía de Castro, auténtica configuradora de nuestra percepción del territorio. Ella articula una triple reivindicación: lingüística, paisajística y antropológica. Reivindica la dignidad de la lengua, del territorio y del pueblo que lo habita. Por eso busca para los paisajes gallegos referentes prestigiosos de la época, como los de Suiza o Italia. Esa revalorización comienza ya en el prólogo de Cantares Gallegos. Más adelante, Ramón Otero Pedrayo retoma esa línea descriptiva y la profundiza, incorporando el valor simbólico del mapa del [geógrafo] Domingo Fontán que inserta en una escena central de [su novela] Arredor de si, hasta convertirlo en un emblema cultural. Ese mapa tuvo, por ejemplo, un profundo valor simbólico para la emigración, ya que en él aparecían representadas todas las parroquias del país, lo que generaba un fuerte vínculo emocional con la tierra. La literatura y la identidad gallegas están profundamente arraigadas en el territorio.

MLS: It’s a phenomenon that also arises in other cultures. In the 19th century, with [the cultural and artistic movement] Romanticism, a profound revaluation of landscapes took place, especially of those in which a human presence was minimal: the most wild and distant from society. That’s why we went from the French park to the English garden, and spaces that until then had been considered hostile — the sea, the desert, the high mountains — began to be valued in the category of the sublime. In Galicia, the person who brought this to pass (in the 19th century) is Rosalía de Castro, truly shaping our perception of territory. She voiced demands in three areas: linguistics, landscape and anthropology. She asserted the dignity of the language, the land and the people who inhabit it. For this reason, she sought out prestigious references of the period for Galician landscapes, such as those of Switzerland and Italy. This revaluation begins in the prologue of Cantares Gallegos (Galician Songs). Later, Ramón Otero Pedrayo takes up that descriptive line and takes it further, incorporating the symbolic value of [geographer] Domingo Fontán’s map that he inserts in a central scene of [his novel] “Arredor de si” (“Circling”), effectively turning it into a cultural emblem. This map, for example, had a profound symbolic value for emigrants, as it depicted all the country’s parishes, creating a strong emotional connection to the land. Galician literature and identity are deeply rooted in territory.

Galician Literature Day arrived as “ahí ven o maio” (“here comes May”), as the song says. Spring declared itself in its most beautiful form with cherry blossoms and cotton fluff flying through the rivers. In Santiago de Compostela, the bar terraces began to fill up, pilgrims with their huge colourful backpacks crowded the historic centre, students in the new town were beginning to savour the holidays after their final exams, and officials in the institutional buildings hurried along their tasks to return home and enjoy the long days.

Few people remembered the rain, that much-maligned counterpoint that lashed the northern Iberian peninsula for months, that exception to the promise of a perpetually sunny Spain. But if the Galician land (as well as its literature) teaches us anything, it is that without that rain, this lush green would not exist.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the cultural and linguistic preservation of the Galician language and literature, emphasizing their role in identity, belonging, and empowerment of a minority community. It discusses institutional and societal efforts to protect and normalize a regional language, which has broad significance for cultural diversity and minority rights. The story is focused, detailed, and illustrates a meaningful positive impact on cultural heritage and identity.
articles.personality_title: Galician literature shapes identity through language and land
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year on May 17, Galicia, a region in Spain, celebrates Galician Literature Day. This day honors the Galician language and its writers. Galician is spoken by about 3 million people, mostly in the northeast of the Iberian Peninsula. The language and literature are important for the community’s identity and culture.

**What happened** – In 2025, the celebration focused on historic female folk singers called cantareiras. An interview with María López Sández, an award-winning Galician writer, explains how literature helps build Galician identity. She says literature is more than art; it is a way for people to feel they belong and understand themselves. López Sández also discussed how institutions can protect the language by using it in schools, government, and everyday life.

**Impact** – Galician culture is unique because it connects deeply with the land and history. Writers like Rosalía de Castro have helped people see the landscape and language as symbols of their identity. The literature and language keep the community connected, even for those living far away. Protecting Galician also supports cultural diversity and shows how minority languages can thrive.

**What's next step** – Efforts will continue to promote Galician in education and public life, following rules like the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages. Digital projects help preserve endangered languages, and learning from places like East Timor shows that supporting many languages is possible. This ongoing work aims to keep Galician alive and strong for future generations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Galician literature and language are powerful tools that connect people to their land, history, and identity, helping preserve a unique culture in today’s world.
articles.personality_title_fr: La littérature galicienne façonne l’identité par la langue et la terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, le 17 mai, la Galice, une région d’Espagne, célèbre la Journée de la littérature galicienne. Cette journée honore la langue galicienne et ses écrivains. Le galicien est parlé par environ 3 millions de personnes, principalement dans le nord-est de la péninsule ibérique. La langue et la littérature sont essentielles pour l’identité et la culture de la communauté.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, la célébration a mis en lumière des chanteuses folkloriques historiques appelées cantareiras. Une interview avec María López Sández, écrivaine galicienne primée, explique comment la littérature aide à construire l’identité galicienne. Elle affirme que la littérature est plus qu’un art ; c’est un moyen pour les gens de se sentir appartenir et de mieux se comprendre. López Sández a aussi parlé de la manière dont les institutions peuvent protéger la langue en l’utilisant dans les écoles, le gouvernement et la vie quotidienne.

**Impact** – La culture galicienne est unique car elle est profondément liée à la terre et à l’histoire. Des écrivains comme Rosalía de Castro ont aidé les gens à voir le paysage et la langue comme des symboles de leur identité. La littérature et la langue maintiennent le lien entre les membres de la communauté, même ceux qui vivent loin. Protéger le galicien soutient aussi la diversité culturelle et montre que les langues minoritaires peuvent prospérer.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les efforts vont continuer pour promouvoir le galicien dans l’éducation et la vie publique, en suivant des règles comme la Charte européenne des langues régionales ou minoritaires. Des projets numériques aident à préserver les langues en danger, et l’exemple du Timor oriental montre qu’il est possible de soutenir plusieurs langues. Ce travail vise à garder le galicien vivant et fort pour les générations futures.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La littérature et la langue galiciennes sont des outils puissants qui relient les gens à leur terre, leur histoire et leur identité, contribuant à préserver une culture unique dans le monde d’aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: La literatura gallega moldea la identidad a través de la lengua y la tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, el 17 de mayo, Galicia, una región de España, celebra el Día de la Literatura Gallega. Este día honra la lengua gallega y a sus escritores. El gallego es hablado por unas 3 millones de personas, principalmente en el noreste de la península ibérica. La lengua y la literatura son fundamentales para la identidad y la cultura de la comunidad.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, la celebración se centró en cantareiras, cantantes folclóricas históricas. Una entrevista con María López Sández, escritora gallega premiada, explica cómo la literatura ayuda a construir la identidad gallega. Ella dice que la literatura es más que arte; es una forma para que las personas sientan pertenencia y se entiendan a sí mismas. López Sández también habló de cómo las instituciones pueden proteger la lengua usándola en escuelas, gobierno y la vida diaria.

**Impacto** – La cultura gallega es única porque está profundamente conectada con la tierra y la historia. Escritores como Rosalía de Castro ayudaron a que la gente vea el paisaje y la lengua como símbolos de su identidad. La literatura y la lengua mantienen unidos a los miembros de la comunidad, incluso a los que viven lejos. Proteger el gallego también apoya la diversidad cultural y muestra que las lenguas minoritarias pueden prosperar.

**Próximos pasos** – Los esfuerzos continuarán para promover el gallego en la educación y la vida pública, siguiendo reglas como la Carta Europea de las Lenguas Regionales o Minoritarias. Proyectos digitales ayudan a preservar lenguas en peligro, y el ejemplo de Timor Oriental muestra que es posible apoyar muchas lenguas. Este trabajo busca mantener el gallego vivo y fuerte para las generaciones futuras.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La literatura y la lengua gallegas son herramientas poderosas que conectan a las personas con su tierra, historia e identidad, ayudando a preservar una cultura única en el mundo actual.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a lush, green Galician landscape under gentle spring rain, featuring symbolic tambourines resting beside blooming cherry blossoms and drifting cotton fluff over winding rivers, with faint, abstract silhouettes of traditional folk singers represented by softly glowing, intertwined musical notes rising from the land.

articles.title: What’s behind China’s historically high counts of corresponding authors?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01623-4From 2016 to 2020, almost one-third of papers by authors in China had multiple corresponding authors — but policy reforms are expected to have dampened the trend.
articles.content:
China’s previous evaluation system rewarded researchers who were listed as corresponding authors.Credit: Andrii Yalanskyi/iStock via Getty

Researchers in China were up to nine times more likely, between 2016 and 2020, to list multiple corresponding authors on a paper than were academics in other countries, according to a study that tracked authorship trends over that period.

The analysis, published in Scientometrics1, by a team of researchers in China, examined nearly 1.75 million articles indexed by Clarivate’s Web of Science over the five-year period to assess how common papers with multiple corresponding authors are around the world.

The results show that in 2020, around 30% of papers by researchers in China had more than one corresponding author — nearly three times the global average. That figure increased from 21.6% in 2016. The global average, by comparison, was relatively stable over the period, increasing from 7.1% to 8.8%.

In academic publishing, the corresponding-author position is typically assigned to the person who is responsible for managing communications with journal editors and handling all manuscript-related correspondence after publication. They might also be responsible for administration work such as paying any publishing fees. A corresponding author can double as the first author, the last author or any co-author, depending on how the research team assigns roles.

Researchers in Singapore and South Korea were also found to have high proportions of papers with multiple corresponding authors, both at just under 20% for the 2016–20 period. When papers from China, Singapore and South Korea were excluded from the data set, the average share fell to just over 7.5%.

The disparities between China and other countries differed depending on the discipline. In medicine and pharmacology, for instance, researchers from China were roughly nine times more likely to list more than one corresponding author relative to the global baseline (excluding South Korea and Singapore), whereas in humanities and social sciences, that figure was around five times.

Assigning credit

In big, collaborative research projects, multiple corresponding authors can reflect genuine shared leadership and responsibility, says Wencan Tian, a social scientist at Beijing Normal University in Zhuhai and a co-author of the study.

However, the high value placed on particular authorship positions in some countries’ research-evaluation systems can make corresponding authorships vulnerable to misuse.

The evaluation system that was in place in China when the study was undertaken placed particular emphasis on first- and corresponding-author positions when it came to deciding who would get promotions, financial rewards and access to research funding, says Tian, which might explain the results of the analysis.

China has since reformed this system, and it is now closer to “internationally recognized principles that emphasize substantive contribution, peer evaluation and differentiated assessment”, says Tian.

These reforms reduce the likelihood of researchers using authorship roles “as mere tools for status distribution”, he adds.

China’s research evaluation revamp should not mean fewer international collaborations

Shifting incentives

In early 2020, China’s education and science ministries issued a joint official statement that institutions should stop promoting or recruiting researchers solely on the basis of number of papers or citations. The move prompted a major shift away from using publication records in the Science Citation Index (SCI), an international database of articles and citation records run by analytics firm Clarivate, based in London, as a means to evaluate and rank the country’s researchers and institutions2.

Subsequently, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC), a governmental agency that funds basic research, ended a policy that paid cash rewards to researchers for having a paper published. It also implemented stricter measures against gift authorship, a practice in which researchers are added as co-authors without contributing much or any work to the paper.

The NSFC now requires that researchers explicitly state their contributions to papers and strictly prohibits authors from lying about their contributions or exaggerating their roles, says Tian. “Such rules are instrumental in curbing irregular authorship practices and ensuring that authorship reverts to its core functions: reflecting academic responsibility and actual contribution.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on China's reform of its research evaluation system to reduce misuse of authorship credit, promoting fairness, academic integrity, and responsible research practices. These policy changes have broad significance for the global academic community and improve the quality and ethics of scientific contributions, which benefits society by fostering trustworthy knowledge production.
articles.personality_title: China changes how researchers share credit to improve fairness
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** –
Between 2016 and 2020, researchers in China listed multiple corresponding authors on nearly one-third of their scientific papers, a much higher rate than other countries. The corresponding author is the person who manages communication with journals and often holds a key role in the research.

**What happened** –
A study examined 1.75 million papers worldwide and found that in China, about 30% of papers had more than one corresponding author in 2020, up from 21.6% in 2016. This was nearly three times the global average. Researchers in Singapore and South Korea also showed higher rates, but less than China. The trend was especially strong in fields like medicine and pharmacology.

**Impact** –
This high number of multiple corresponding authors was partly due to China’s old research evaluation system, which rewarded researchers who held these positions with promotions and funding. This encouraged listing multiple corresponding authors even if they didn’t all contribute equally. China has now reformed this system to focus more on actual work done and peer review. These changes aim to stop unfair practices like gift authorship, where people get credit without real contribution.

**What's next step** –
China’s new rules require researchers to clearly state their contributions and prevent exaggerating roles. This is expected to make authorship fairer and more honest. It may also encourage better international cooperation by aligning China’s practices with global standards.

**One-sentence takeaway** –
China is improving how it credits researchers to ensure fairness and honesty in scientific work.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine réforme le partage des crédits de recherche pour plus d’équité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** –
Entre 2016 et 2020, les chercheurs en Chine ont indiqué plusieurs auteurs correspondants sur près d’un tiers de leurs articles scientifiques, un taux beaucoup plus élevé que dans d’autres pays. L’auteur correspondant est la personne qui gère la communication avec les revues et joue souvent un rôle clé dans la recherche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** –
Une étude a examiné 1,75 million d’articles dans le monde et a révélé qu’en Chine, environ 30 % des articles avaient plus d’un auteur correspondant en 2020, contre 21,6 % en 2016. C’est presque trois fois la moyenne mondiale. Les chercheurs de Singapour et de Corée du Sud ont aussi des taux élevés, mais inférieurs à ceux de la Chine. Cette tendance est particulièrement forte en médecine et pharmacologie.

**Impact** –
Ce nombre élevé d’auteurs correspondants multiples était en partie dû à l’ancien système d’évaluation de la recherche en Chine, qui récompensait les chercheurs occupant ces postes par des promotions et des financements. Cela encourageait à inscrire plusieurs auteurs correspondants même s’ils ne contribuaient pas tous de manière égale. La Chine a maintenant réformé ce système pour se concentrer davantage sur le travail réel accompli et l’évaluation par les pairs. Ces changements visent à stopper les pratiques injustes comme la co-signature cadeau, où des personnes reçoivent un crédit sans réelle contribution.

**Prochaines étapes** –
Les nouvelles règles chinoises obligent les chercheurs à préciser clairement leurs contributions et interdisent d’exagérer leur rôle. Cela devrait rendre la paternité plus juste et honnête. Cela pourrait aussi encourager une meilleure coopération internationale en alignant les pratiques chinoises sur les normes mondiales.

**Message clé en une phrase** –
La Chine améliore la manière dont elle attribue les crédits de recherche pour garantir équité et honnêteté dans le travail scientifique.
articles.personality_title_es: China cambia cómo se comparte el crédito de investigación para mejorar la justicia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** –
Entre 2016 y 2020, los investigadores en China pusieron múltiples autores correspondientes en casi un tercio de sus artículos científicos, una tasa mucho más alta que en otros países. El autor correspondiente es la persona que maneja la comunicación con las revistas y suele tener un papel importante en la investigación.

**Qué pasó** –
Un estudio analizó 1,75 millones de artículos en todo el mundo y encontró que en China, alrededor del 30 % de los artículos tenían más de un autor correspondiente en 2020, frente al 21,6 % en 2016. Esto es casi tres veces el promedio mundial. Los investigadores de Singapur y Corea del Sur también mostraron tasas altas, pero menores que China. La tendencia fue especialmente fuerte en medicina y farmacología.

**Impacto** –
Este alto número de autores correspondientes múltiples se debió en parte al antiguo sistema chino de evaluación de la investigación, que premiaba a los investigadores en estas posiciones con promociones y fondos. Esto animaba a poner varios autores correspondientes aunque no todos contribuyeran igual. China ha reformado este sistema para centrarse más en el trabajo real y la revisión por pares. Estos cambios buscan detener prácticas injustas como la autoría de regalo, donde se da crédito sin verdadera contribución.

**Próximo paso** –
Las nuevas reglas chinas exigen que los investigadores expliquen claramente sus contribuciones y prohíben exagerar sus roles. Se espera que esto haga la autoría más justa y honesta. También puede fomentar mejor cooperación internacional al alinear las prácticas de China con los estándares globales.

**Resumen en una frase** –
China está mejorando cómo acredita a los investigadores para asegurar justicia y honestidad en el trabajo científico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a harmonious circle of glowing paper scrolls and ink brushes arranged like a collaborative team around a softly lit traditional Chinese scholar’s desk, connected by delicate golden threads symbolizing shared authorship and evolving academic integrity, rendered in natural earthy tones and gentle pastels.
